food_subgroup: Seaweed and sea vegetables — braised kelp preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kelp food product class; no specific entry for Beijing-style braised kelp exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers seaweeds and algae as a category. No specific braised kelp preparation code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised kelp or Beijing-style kelp preparation. Null assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Beijing-style braised kelp is approximately 150 g, served as a cold or warm side dish alongside rice or other mains.

nutrient_source.title: Kelp, raw (Laminaria spp.) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11445; adapted for braised preparation with soy sauce and sesame oil seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw kelp nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168457) were used as the base. Values were adjusted upward for sodium (reflecting soy sauce addition, estimated ~400–500 mg/100 g in finished dish), and minor upward adjustment for fat and energy to reflect sesame oil and cooking oil. Carbohydrate and fibre values retained from raw kelp baseline. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seaweed and kelp-based foods — analogous estimate based on published GI values for kelp and mixed seaweed preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style braised kelp. Kelp and seaweed-based foods are consistently reported in the low-GI range (GI approximately 15–22) in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature, reflecting their high water content, low digestible carbohydrate, and high soluble fibre (alginate). A GI of 17 is used as a defensible analogous estimate. GL per 150 g serving = (17 × 9.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.3, confirming very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~480 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Beijing-style braised kelp in moderation due to its elevated sodium content from soy sauce; requesting a reduced-sodium preparation or limiting portion size to 75–100 g per meal is advisable to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~17) and low digestible carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beijing-style braised kelp is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving; the soluble fibre (alginate) in kelp may additionally help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in potassium, magnesium, and omega-3 fatty acids; low in saturated fat; however sodium from soy sauce warrants attention
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kelp provides cardioprotective minerals including potassium (89 mg/100 g) and magnesium (121 mg/100 g) and is low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the soy sauce-derived sodium and opt for low-sodium soy sauce preparations where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing-Style Braised Kelp (京派酥海带, Jīng pài sū hǎidài) is a traditional Chinese cold or warm side dish originating from Beijing cuisine. It is prepared by braising strips of dried or fresh kelp (Laminaria japonica) in a seasoned liquid comprising soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and vinegar, resulting in a tender, slightly chewy, and deeply savoury product with a characteristic umami flavour profile. The dish is prized for its nutritional density: kelp is one of the richest dietary sources of iodine, and also provides calcium (168 mg/100 g), magnesium (121 mg/100 g), iron (2.8 mg/100 g), and soluble dietary fibre in the form of alginate. The energy content is modest at approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, making it a low-calorie, nutrient-dense accompaniment. Its very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~17) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) contributed by soy sauce seasoning, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese restaurants and food delivery platforms as a cold appetiser or side dish. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, and is free of dairy and gluten-containing grains (though soy sauce contains wheat in most formulations). The dish is also known as crispy braised kelp or Beijing kelp, and is a staple of Beijing-style cold appetiser platters.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in braised kelp?
What are the health benefits of kelp?
Is 京派酥海带 gluten free?
Can hypertension patients eat braised kelp?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Beijing-style braised kelp was found in any approved primary source. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw kelp data (FDC ID 168457) with estimated adjustments for soy sauce sodium contribution and added oil. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on seaweed/kelp category values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw kelp (FDC ID 168457) with upward sodium adjustment (~480 mg/100 g) to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this preparation. Energy and fat values adjusted upward to reflect sesame oil and cooking oil addition. 2. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy kelp entry for this preparation context. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as kelp is a plant-derived food and B12 is not reliably present; not reported in the source. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for kelp. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy kelp entry. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA source for kelp and plant oils. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients. 10. GI of 17 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database seaweed/kelp category; no direct measurement for Beijing-style braised kelp exists. 11. Soy sauce typically contains wheat; the dish is therefore not certified gluten-free unless tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is used. 12. Iodine content of kelp is notably high (kelp can contain 1500–8000 mcg iodine per 100 g dry weight); individuals with thyroid conditions should consult a clinician before regular consumption. Iodine_mcg field is not present in the template and is noted here for clinical awareness.
